Group A — Yogurt Study: Final Presentation

Author

Workshop Participant

1. What biological question did you investigate?

Does eating yogurt help Lactobacillus crispatus — the protective vaginal bacterium — recover after antibiotic treatment?

Antibiotics disrupt the vaginal microbiome by killing bacteria indiscriminately. We wanted to know if eating yogurt (which contains bacteria) helps L. crispatus bounce back faster compared to an unchanged diet.

2. Who are the participants?

Code 
participants |>
  select(-pid, -sex) |>
  tbl_summary(
    by = arm,
    statistic = list(age ~ "{mean} ({sd})")
  ) |>
  add_p() |>
  add_overall() |>
  bold_labels() |>
  modify_header(label = "**Characteristic**")
Characteristic Overall
N = 511		 unchanged_diet
N = 251		 yogurt
N = 261		 p-value2
days_since_last_sex 5 (3, 11) 6 (3, 10) 5 (3, 11) 0.8
birth_control


0.017
    Depoprovera 25 (49%) 8 (32%) 17 (65%)
    no hormonal birth control 26 (51%) 17 (68%) 9 (35%)
age 30.7 (3.3) 30.4 (3.3) 31.0 (3.3) 0.6
education


0.8
    grade 10-12, matriculated 10 (20%) 6 (24%) 4 (15%)
    grade 10-12, not matriculated 21 (41%) 9 (36%) 12 (46%)
    less than grade 9 9 (18%) 4 (16%) 5 (19%)
    post-secondary 11 (22%) 6 (24%) 5 (19%)
1 Median (Q1, Q3); n (%); Mean (SD)
2 Wilcoxon rank sum test; Pearson’s Chi-squared test; Fisher’s exact test

Key takeaways:

  • 51 participants (25 unchanged diet, 26 yogurt) — roughly balanced
  • Age similar across groups (mean ≈ 31 in both, p = 0.55)
  • But birth control is imbalanced: 17 of 26 yogurt participants use Depoprovera vs. only 8 of 25 in the unchanged diet group. This could matter for the microbiome.

3. What did you compare, and what did you find?

Main finding: L. crispatus recovery after antibiotics

In the yogurt group, 85% of samples had detectable L. crispatus after antibiotics. In the unchanged diet group, only 48% did.

Code 
# Prepare paired data for L. crispatus
qpcr_joined |>
  filter(time_point %in% c("baseline", "after_antibiotic")) |>
  mutate(time_point = fct_relevel(time_point, "baseline", "after_antibiotic")) |>
  ggplot(aes(time_point, qpcr_crispatus)) +
  geom_line(aes(group = pid), alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_point(aes(color = arm), size = 2.5, alpha = 0.7) +
  facet_wrap(~ arm) +
  scale_y_log10(labels = scales::comma) +
  labs(
    title = "L. crispatus: baseline → after antibiotic (each line = one person)",
    subtitle = "Yogurt group shows more recovery after antibiotics",
    y = "L. crispatus DNA copies (log scale)",
    x = "",
    color = "Group"
  ) +
  stat_compare_means(
    method = "wilcox.test",
    paired = TRUE,
    comparisons = list(c("baseline", "after_antibiotic"))
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

What this shows: Each line is one participant. At baseline, almost everyone has near-zero L. crispatus in both groups. After antibiotics, the yogurt group has many participants with visible L. crispatus levels, while the unchanged diet group mostly stays flat at zero.

Secondary finding: Total bacterial load

Code 
paired_bacteria <- qpcr_joined |>
  select(pid, arm, time_point, qpcr_bacteria) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = time_point, values_from = qpcr_bacteria)

paired_bacteria |>
  ggplot(aes(x = "Baseline", y = baseline)) +
  geom_point(aes(color = arm), size = 2.5, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = "Baseline", xend = "After antibiotic",
                   y = baseline, yend = after_antibiotic,
                   color = arm, group = pid),
               alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_point(aes(x = "After antibiotic", y = after_antibiotic, color = arm),
             size = 2.5, alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_y_log10(labels = scales::comma) +
  facet_wrap(~ arm) +
  labs(
    title = "Total bacterial load: each participant's change",
    subtitle = "Yogurt group tends to decrease; unchanged diet is mixed",
    y = "Total bacterial DNA copies (log scale)",
    x = "",
    color = "Group"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

What this shows: Most participants in the yogurt group show a decrease in total bacterial load from baseline to after antibiotics. The unchanged diet group is more mixed — about half go up, half go down.

4. Limitations

What could go wrong with our conclusion?

  1. Birth control imbalance — 65% of the yogurt group uses Depoprovera vs. 32% of the unchanged diet group. Depoprovera affects hormones and could independently influence the vaginal microbiome. The L. crispatus “recovery” might partly reflect this difference, not the yogurt.

  2. No placebo — participants knew which group they were in. We can’t rule out placebo effects or behavioural changes.

  3. L. crispatus mostly absent at baseline — with 96% of samples showing zero at baseline, we’re comparing recovery from near-zero, which is hard to measure precisely.

  4. Small sample — 51 participants is modest. One or two extreme values can influence the results.

  5. Out-of-range cytokine measurements — several cytokines (IL-10, IFN-Y, TNFa) were almost entirely below detection limits, so we couldn’t assess immune response for those.

5. If you had another week, what would you do next?

  1. Adjust for Depoprovera — run a model that controls for birth control type to see if the yogurt effect holds up
  2. Look at L. iners — does yogurt push the microbiome toward L. crispatus dominance and away from L. iners?
  3. Check the usable cytokines (IL-1a, IL-8, IP-10, MIG, MIP-3a) — is there a difference in inflammation between groups?
  4. Identify the 9 extreme total bacteria samples — are they from the same participants? Do they cluster in one group?
  5. Build a simple model predicting L. crispatus recovery from group + age + birth control

6. What did your group learn this week?

  • Data mapping first: Understanding what one row means in each table and how tables connect saved us from making mistakes later
  • Log scales are not optional: When data spans 4+ orders of magnitude (like bacterial DNA counts), raw-scale plots hide everything
  • Zeros can be meaningful: 65% of samples had zero L. crispatus — that’s not missing data, that’s biology
  • “Out of range” ≠ unusable: Many cytokine measurements were below detection limits, which tells us the assay may not be sensitive enough for vaginal samples
  • Making one plot three times taught us more than making three different plots once — each iteration revealed something new

Bonus: Shortlist of biological questions for Thursday

  1. Does yogurt restore L. crispatus after antibiotics, controlling for Depoprovera? (Our main finding, but needs adjustment)
  2. Does the yogurt group have a different L. crispatus / L. iners ratio after antibiotics? (A more stable microbiome has more L. crispatus)
  3. Is total bacterial load reduction after antibiotics beneficial or harmful? (More bacteria isn’t always better)
  4. Do baseline characteristics (age, birth control, days since last sex) predict microbiome recovery regardless of group?
  5. Are the extreme total bacteria values real biological variation or sample contamination? (Samples >29 million copies need investigation)
